questions.question: Section 39 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018 created the offence of coercive control. What are the essential elements of this offence?

options.A: The accused must commit at least one act of physical violence; persistent psychological conduct alone cannot constitute coercive control
options.B: The offence is committed where a person knowingly and persistently engages in behaviour that is controlling or coercive towards a person with whom they are or were in an intimate relationship, and that behaviour has a serious effect on the person; no single act of physical violence is required — the offence captures patterns of non-physical controlling and coercive conduct
options.C: The offence requires proof that the accused made explicit threats of violence — coercive control cannot be established by subtle or implied threats
options.D: The offence applies only during the subsistence of a relationship — conduct occurring after the relationship has ended cannot constitute coercive control

questions.explanation: The offence of coercive control under s.39 Domestic Violence Act 2018 has three essential elements: (1) the behaviour must be 'knowingly and persistently' controlling or coercive — a single incident is insufficient; the offence captures patterns of behaviour; (2) the parties must be in or have been in an intimate relationship — the offence applies to intimate partners; and (3) the behaviour must have a 'serious effect' on the victim — this is a threshold requirement preventing minor or trivial controlling behaviour from being criminalised. The critical feature of coercive control is that it captures non-physical forms of abuse: financial control, psychological manipulation, isolation from family and friends, monitoring of movements, control of appearance and activities. The offence was designed to address the reality that domestic abuse is frequently non-physical. Option A incorrectly requires physical violence. Option C incorrectly limits the offence to explicit threats. Option D incorrectly excludes post-relationship conduct — the Act covers persons who 'are or were' in an intimate relationship.

questions.question: What is the maximum sentence for the offence of coercive control under s.39 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018?

options.D: Life imprisonment — reflecting the gravity of the offence

questions.explanation: The maximum sentence for the offence of coercive control under s.39 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018 is 5 years imprisonment on indictment. This places coercive control in a category of serious indictable offences, reflecting the legislature's view that persistent patterns of controlling and coercive behaviour cause significant harm to victims. The 5-year maximum provides sentencing courts with appropriate range to reflect the gravity and duration of coercive control conduct. Summary disposal is also available for less serious cases. FE-1 candidates must know the maximum sentence: 5 years on indictment. This is a common exam detail. Options A and C state incorrect maximum sentences. Option D is entirely incorrect — life imprisonment is not provided for coercive control.

questions.question: In DPP v Dunleavy [2020] (Ireland), one of the first coercive control prosecutions in Ireland, the accused had made over 6,000 phone calls to the victim. What does this case illustrate about the offence of coercive control?

options.A: The case established that the number of calls is the only relevant metric — at least 1,000 calls are required for a coercive control conviction
options.B: DPP v Dunleavy [2020] illustrates the 'persistence' element of coercive control — the enormous volume of calls (over 6,000) demonstrated a pattern of controlling behaviour that the court treated as falling within s.39 DVA 2018; the case is significant as one of the first successful prosecutions under the new provision and demonstrates how the courts approached the persistence requirement in practice
options.C: The case established that coercive control by telephone communication is a separate offence from face-to-face coercive control and requires different proof
options.D: The case illustrated that the offence of coercive control cannot be established without additional evidence of physical violence alongside the phone calls

questions.explanation: DPP v Dunleavy [2020] is an important early example of a coercive control prosecution in Ireland. The case involved over 6,000 phone calls made by the accused to the victim — a pattern of conduct that clearly demonstrated the 'knowingly and persistently' element of the s.39 DVA 2018 offence. The case is significant as: (1) one of the first successful prosecutions for coercive control following the commencement of the DVA 2018; (2) it demonstrates how courts assess the 'persistence' requirement in practice; and (3) it shows that coercive control does not require face-to-face contact — controlling conduct through persistent communication can satisfy the offence. The Dunleavy case illustrates the legislature's intent to capture systematic controlling behaviour that causes serious harm to victims. Option A incorrectly creates a numerical threshold of calls. Option C incorrectly creates a separate offence for phone-based coercive control. Option D incorrectly requires physical violence in addition.

questions.question: In DPP v Kane [2023] IECA 86 (Ireland), the Court of Appeal considered the scope of the coercive control offence. What principle regarding the type of conduct captured by s.39 did Kennedy J confirm?

options.A: Kennedy J held that coercive control is limited to financial abuse and physical threats — emotional or psychological abuse without financial or physical components cannot constitute the offence
options.B: Kennedy J confirmed that s.39 DVA 2018 captures emotional and psychological abuse — persistent patterns of psychological control, emotional manipulation, and humiliation that have a serious effect on the victim fall within the scope of the coercive control offence; it is not limited to physical acts or financial control
options.C: Kennedy J held that coercive control requires a formal expert diagnosis of psychological harm to the victim — lay evidence of the serious effect is insufficient
options.D: Kennedy J held that the offence can only be established where there is corroborating evidence beyond the victim's testimony alone

questions.explanation: DPP v Kane [2023] IECA 86 is an important appellate authority confirming the broad scope of the coercive control offence. Kennedy J in the Court of Appeal confirmed that s.39 DVA 2018 captures emotional and psychological abuse — the offence is not limited to financial abuse or physical threats. Persistent patterns of psychological control, emotional manipulation, humiliation, isolation, and monitoring of the victim's life can constitute coercive control where they have a serious effect on the victim. This confirmation is important because it ensures that the offence achieves its intended purpose of capturing the full spectrum of domestic abuse conduct, not just the more obvious physical and financial forms. The Kane decision is authority for the proposition that purely psychological controlling conduct can satisfy s.39. Option A incorrectly limits the offence to financial and physical threats. Option C incorrectly requires formal expert diagnosis. Option D incorrectly requires corroboration beyond the victim's testimony.

questions.question: In DPP v Jake Boles [2023] IECA (Ireland), Edwards J delivered a norm-setting judgment on sentencing for coercive control. What was the significance of the Mahoney principle referenced in this case?

options.A: The Mahoney principle requires that coercive control sentences must always be suspended on first offence — custodial sentences are unavailable for first-time offenders
options.B: DPP v Jake Boles [2023] IECA saw Edwards J deliver a norm-setting judgment on coercive control sentencing; the Mahoney principle (drawn from DPP v Mahoney) is the principle of sentencing guidance applied by the Court of Appeal to establish appropriate ranges for coercive control offences, providing courts with structured guidance on where within the sentencing range different types and gravities of coercive control conduct should fall
options.C: The Mahoney principle holds that coercive control must always be sentenced consecutively to any associated physical assault charges — consecutive sentencing is mandatory
options.D: The Mahoney principle limits coercive control sentences to 12 months regardless of the seriousness of the conduct, pending legislative review

questions.explanation: DPP v Jake Boles [2023] IECA is a landmark sentencing case for coercive control offences in Ireland. Edwards J delivered a norm-setting judgment — a structured guidance judgment that sets out how the sentencing range for coercive control offences should be applied across different levels of gravity. The Mahoney principle, derived from the broader appellate jurisprudence on sentencing guidance (DPP v Mahoney and related cases), is the approach of the Court of Appeal to establishing structured sentencing ranges for categories of offences to promote consistency and proportionality. In Boles, Edwards J applied this approach to coercive control — mapping out how factors such as the duration of the conduct, the severity of the harm, and the vulnerability of the victim should affect the sentence within the 5-year maximum. The Boles judgment gives practitioners and judges a framework for sentencing coercive control cases. Option A invents a suspended sentence rule. Option C invents a mandatory consecutive sentencing rule. Option D invents a 12-month cap.

questions.question: Adam and Beth were in an intimate relationship for three years. During the relationship, Adam systematically monitored Beth's phone, controlled who she could see, restricted her access to money, and repeatedly humiliated her in front of others. The relationship ended six months ago. Can Adam be prosecuted for coercive control under s.39 DVA 2018?

options.A: No — s.39 DVA 2018 only applies to ongoing relationships; once the relationship has ended, the coercive control offence can no longer be established for past conduct
options.B: Yes — s.39 DVA 2018 applies to persons who 'are or were' in an intimate relationship; conduct occurring during the relationship can be the subject of a prosecution even after the relationship has ended; the offence is established by the conduct during the relationship and the serious effect on Beth, regardless of the relationship's current status
options.C: Yes — but only if the controlling conduct continued after the relationship ended; conduct that occurred exclusively during the relationship cannot be prosecuted after its conclusion
options.D: Yes — but Adam can only be prosecuted for the financial control aspects; monitoring, isolation, and humiliation are not within the statutory definition of coercive control

questions.explanation: Adam can be prosecuted for coercive control under s.39 DVA 2018 even though the relationship has ended. The statute expressly extends to persons who 'are or were' in an intimate relationship — this was a deliberate legislative choice to ensure that the ending of a relationship does not provide immunity from prosecution for conduct that occurred during it. The elements of the offence are satisfied: (1) the conduct was knowing and persistent (systematic monitoring, financial control, isolation, humiliation over three years); (2) Adam and Beth were in an intimate relationship; and (3) the conduct had a serious effect on Beth. The fact that the relationship has since ended does not prevent prosecution for the past conduct. This extension to former relationships is important — victims often leave relationships before reporting coercive control, and the law must protect their ability to pursue justice. Option A incorrectly limits the offence to ongoing relationships. Option C incorrectly requires post-relationship conduct. Option D incorrectly limits the statutory scope to financial control.

questions.question: What does 'serious effect' mean in the context of the coercive control offence under s.39 DVA 2018?

options.A: 'Serious effect' requires medical evidence of a diagnosed psychiatric condition caused by the controlling behaviour — without clinical evidence of harm, the threshold is not met
options.B: 'Serious effect' refers to the impact the controlling or coercive behaviour has on the victim — it captures effects such as substantial adverse impact on daily life and activities, causing the victim to fear for their safety, or causing mental or physical harm; the threshold prevents trivial or minor impacts from founding the offence while capturing genuine and significant harm
options.C: 'Serious effect' is a purely subjective assessment — any effect that the victim personally considers serious satisfies the threshold, regardless of whether a reasonable person would regard it as serious
options.D: 'Serious effect' requires that the victim have made a complaint to the Gardaí or a health professional at the time the conduct occurred — retrospective evidence of effect is insufficient

questions.explanation: The 'serious effect' element of the coercive control offence under s.39 DVA 2018 is a threshold requirement that ensures the offence captures genuine harm without over-criminalising trivial conduct. 'Serious effect' encompasses: substantial adverse effects on the victim's daily life and activities; causing the victim to fear for their safety; and causing mental or physical harm. The legislation (and the case law following, including DPP v Kane [2023]) makes clear that this includes psychological and emotional harm — not just physical injury or medically diagnosed conditions. The 'serious effect' element is assessed having regard to all the circumstances, including the cumulative effect of the pattern of behaviour. Option A incorrectly requires a clinical diagnosis. Option C incorrectly makes the test entirely subjective by reference to the victim's personal assessment. Option D incorrectly requires contemporaneous complaints to Gardaí or health professionals.

questions.question: How does the offence of coercive control under s.39 DVA 2018 differ from the pre-existing offence of harassment under s.10 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997?

options.A: They are essentially the same offence — coercive control is simply a rename of harassment in the domestic context
options.B: The offences differ in several ways: (1) s.39 DVA 2018 coercive control requires an intimate relationship between the parties — s.10 NFOATP 1997 harassment applies to any persons; (2) coercive control specifically captures controlling or coercive behaviour patterns in intimate relationships (financial control, isolation, psychological manipulation) which go beyond the 'causing alarm, distress, or harm' formulation of harassment; (3) coercive control addresses a broader pattern of abuse over time rather than repetitive individual acts of harassment
options.C: Harassment under s.10 NFOATP 1997 is more serious than coercive control — harassment carries a higher maximum penalty
options.D: The only difference is the penalty — coercive control and harassment are substantively identical offences

questions.explanation: Coercive control under s.39 DVA 2018 and harassment under s.10 NFOATP 1997 are distinct offences addressing different (though sometimes overlapping) conduct. Key differences include: (1) Relationship requirement — coercive control specifically requires an intimate relationship between the parties; harassment under s.10 can apply between any persons; (2) Type of conduct — s.39 specifically addresses controlling and coercive patterns of behaviour in intimate relationships including financial control, isolation, monitoring, and psychological manipulation; s.10 harassment captures persistent conduct that causes alarm, distress, or harm; (3) Design — coercive control was specifically designed to address the phenomenon of domestic abuse as a pattern of control, not merely repeated harassing acts; and (4) Maximum sentence — s.39 coercive control carries a maximum of 5 years on indictment; s.10 harassment carries a maximum of 7 years (as amended). The two offences can potentially overlap but address distinct social harms. Options A and D incorrectly treat them as the same. Option C incorrectly compares the penalties.

questions.question: Claire's partner David controls all household finances, prevents her from seeing her friends and family, monitors her every movement through location-sharing apps, dictates what she can wear, and constantly humiliates her in front of others. Claire has never been physically assaulted. Can David be prosecuted for coercive control?

options.A: No — coercive control requires at least one act of physical violence; since David has never physically assaulted Claire, the offence cannot be established
options.B: Yes — the s.39 DVA 2018 offence does not require any physical violence; David's conduct (financial control, isolation from family and friends, surveillance through location apps, controlling appearance, and psychological humiliation) constitutes a pattern of knowingly and persistently controlling and coercive behaviour that has a serious effect on Claire; this is precisely the type of conduct the offence was designed to capture
options.C: Yes — but only the financial control element satisfies the offence; monitoring, controlling appearance, and social isolation are not within the statutory definition
options.D: No — the use of location-sharing apps (which Claire may have agreed to) prevents the conduct from being classified as coercive — technological surveillance in a relationship is not within the scope of s.39

questions.explanation: David can be prosecuted for coercive control under s.39 DVA 2018. The scenario describes precisely the type of conduct the offence was designed to address — a pattern of non-physical abuse that comprehensively controls every aspect of Claire's life. The elements are satisfied: (1) Knowing and persistent — the conduct is systematic and ongoing; (2) Intimate relationship — David and Claire are partners; (3) Controlling or coercive behaviour — financial control, isolation, surveillance, control of appearance, humiliation are all characteristic forms of coercive control; (4) Serious effect — the totality of this level of control clearly has a serious effect on Claire's daily life and autonomy. The absence of physical violence is irrelevant — coercive control was specifically created to address the reality that the most damaging forms of domestic abuse are often non-physical. As confirmed in DPP v Kane [2023], emotional and psychological abuse falls squarely within the offence. Option A incorrectly requires physical violence. Option C incorrectly limits the offence to financial control. Option D incorrectly excludes technological surveillance from the offence.
